glossaryTerms.definition: A low-tech firing method where pots are buried in a pit or metal container with combustible materials and burned in the open air. Temperatures reach roughly 600-900 degrees C.
A protective container that surrounds pottery during firing to control the local atmosphere around the piece.
A crystal-structure change that occurs in silica at 573 degrees C during heating and cooling. Rapid temperature change through this point can crack clay.
Any firing method where combustion gases and smoke make direct contact with the ware, producing unpredictable surface marks and colour variations.
Rubbing the surface of leather-hard or dry clay with a smooth tool or stone to compress and polish the surface, producing a sheen that survives low-temperature firing.

content.text: Pit firing

is one of the oldest methods of hardening clay, and it requires no commercial kiln. You dig a shallow pit, pack the work in with sawdust, newspaper, and wood shavings, light the fire, and let it burn down over several hours. The combustibles create a dense, smoky atmosphere around the pots, leaving behind flashes of black, grey, orange, and brown that no glaze firing can replicate. This is
atmospheric firing
at its most accessible. A galvanised dustbin used as a metal
saggar

works just as well as a dug pit and is easier to manage in a small garden. Temperatures reach approximately 600-900 degrees C, lower than a commercial bisque (typically 1000-1050 degrees C), but sufficient to harden earthenware red clay to a durable decorative finish. The result is not waterproof and not food-safe, but it is striking. Pieces are best left unglazed and finished with
burnishing
before firing to maximise the surface sheen.

attrs.title: Fire safety
attrs.body: Pit firing is an outdoor activity only. Keep the fire at least 5 m from any structure or dry vegetation. Have a bucket of water and a fire blanket within reach. Never leave a live fire unattended.

materials.name: Earthenware red clay (bone-dry pieces)
materials.quantity: as many as fit in a single layer

materials.name: Banana peel or seaweed (for flash marks)

tools.name: Spade or garden trowel (for pit version)
Galvanised metal dustbin with lid (alternative to pit)
Metal rack or bricks for elevating pieces (dustbin version)

content.text: Preparation

Only fire pieces that are completely bone-dry. Any residual moisture will turn to steam and can shatter the work during rapid heating. Earthenware red clay pinch pots, coil forms, and burnished tiles all work well. Burnish the surface with a smooth stone or the back of a spoon while the clay is leather-hard to give the fired piece a polished finish. Let pieces dry for at least 48 hours, longer in humid weather.
Check the
quartz inversion

point: all silica-bearing clay passes through 573 degrees C during heating and cooling. A slow, gradual heat build-up through this range reduces the risk of thermal shock cracking. Keeping the fire temperature low and even in the first 30 minutes (thin newspaper layers at the base, not a bonfire) is the practical equivalent of a slow ramp in a kiln.
Steps

Dig the pit or prepare the bin. For a ground pit, dig 30-40 cm deep and roughly 50 cm wide. For a dustbin saggar, punch 6-8 ventilation holes near the base with a nail and hammer - this draws air and keeps the fire alive without lifting the lid.

Lay a 5 cm base of sawdust in the pit or bin. Place scrunched newspaper on top of the sawdust. This base layer acts as the slow-heat phase: newspaper lights and builds heat gradually before the denser sawdust catches.

Place pieces with care. Set each pot upright or on its side in the sawdust base, leaving a 3-4 cm gap between pieces. Pieces that touch each other or the metal wall may stick or develop pale patches where smoke cannot reach.

Wrap optional combustibles around individual pieces. Wind a strip of banana peel or seaweed around a pot, or dust a small amount of copper carbonate onto the surface for green-to-black colour flashing. The more variation in combustibles, the more varied the marks.

Cover the pieces with a deep layer of sawdust (at least 10 cm) so they are buried. Add wood shavings or dry leaves on top. The idea is a dense smothering mass of combustibles above and below the work.

Ignite the fire. For the pit, light the newspaper at the edge and allow the sawdust to catch progressively. For the dustbin, light newspaper at the top and place the lid on loosely (not sealed) to restrict oxygen and encourage slow smouldering rather than a fast open flame.

Manage the burn. Stay close for the first 30 minutes. If the fire burns too fiercely, restrict airflow by partially covering the pit with a sheet of corrugated metal or tilting the dustbin lid further closed. A long slow smoulder of 2-4 hours gives better results than a fast burn.

Let the fire die completely on its own - do not douse with water. When all combustibles have burned away and the pit or bin is cool (at least 4-6 hours, ideally leave overnight), extract the pieces using metal tongs. The clay will be covered in carbon and ash.
Clean and seal. Brush off loose ash with a dry brush. Wash gently with water. Once dry, apply a thin coat of paste wax or linseed oil to intensify the surface marks and give some protection.
What success looks like

Pieces emerge with a mottled skin of black, grey, and earthy orange. Where combustibles wrapped the pot, there will be sharp-edged dark marks. Burnished surfaces catch the light with a subdued sheen. The clay is hard and rings faintly when tapped. The result is not waterproof, but it is stable and striking.

items.cause: Residual moisture or too-rapid heat build-up through 573 degrees C
items.fix: Ensure bone-dry pieces (48+ hours) and use thin newspaper layers at the base to slow the initial heat ramp.

items.problem: Surface marks are faint - mostly uniform grey
items.cause: Fire burned too fast and hot; smoke contact was brief
items.fix: Restrict airflow more aggressively and bury pieces deeper in sawdust to extend the smoulder time.

items.problem: Pale patches where no marking has occurred
items.cause: Pieces were touching each other or the bin wall, blocking smoke
items.fix: Leave at least 3-4 cm between every piece and at least 2 cm from the metal wall.

items.problem: Fire went out before all combustibles burned
items.cause: Insufficient ventilation holes in the dustbin
items.fix: Add more holes near the base before the next firing - aim for 6-8 holes of about 10 mm diameter.

items.problem: Copper carbonate produced no green - surface is black only
items.cause: Heavy carbon deposit from sawdust has buried the copper colour
items.fix: Use less sawdust directly on top of copper-dusted areas, or apply copper just to one face and pack the other side in heavier combustibles.
